moduleTitle: Service Procedure — Pre-Job Site & Floor Inspection
summary: A structured pre-job walk of a school or childcare site that confirms scope, access, hazards, power and water, child-safe requirements and the condition of every floor in scope. It produces the dated inspection record, SDS readiness and method/scheduling plan a contract expects before you quote or start work.

steps.title: Confirm scope and the current contract specification
steps.detail: Before anything else, read what you are actually being asked to service. Separate routine scope (light, frequent cleaning) from periodic scope (hard-floor strip-and-seal, carpet hot water extraction) and list every surface in play — carpet, tile and grout, vinyl and resilient floors, and external hard surfaces for pressure washing. Frequencies and tasks in published standards such as School Infrastructure NSW are directional only; the binding detail is the current contract specification, so verify against it rather than assuming.

steps.title: Confirm site credentials and child-safe requirements before you attend
steps.detail: Child-related work at schools and childcare centres generally requires a Working With Children check, administered per state and territory (NSW WWCC, VIC Working with Children Check, QLD Blue Card, ACT WWVP registration) and generally not transferable between jurisdictions. Confirm each worker attending holds the correct current check, and complete any site child-safe induction — treat the WWC check as a starting point, not the whole picture. Arrange your sign-in and site contact before arrival.

steps.title: Book the inspection around occupancy
steps.detail: Schedule the inspection — and plan the work — around when children are present. Note the low-occupancy windows and the term-break or school-holiday periods that periodic work will need, and confirm access times with the site contact rather than assuming a window is clear. Booking around occupancy is your first signal to the client that you understand how a child-occupied site runs.

steps.title: Walk the site and map rooms, zones and access
steps.detail: Walk every area in scope. Map the rooms and zones, note occupancy patterns and entry/exit routes, and identify where children spend time so you can plan physical separation of the work zone later. Note where you can sequence the work room-by-room so each space can be treated, dried and reopened while you move on, since you cannot close the whole site at once.

steps.title: Check services and utilities — water and power
steps.detail: Confirm water access for extraction, rinsing and pressure washing, and locate waste-water disposal points. Confirm power is 230 V / 50 Hz through 10 A GPOs protected by an RCD/safety switch, and that every lead and machine you will bring is within its AS/NZS 3760 test-and-tag interval before it comes on site. For pressure washing, locate the stormwater pits and the intended sewer/trade-waste discharge point at this stage, not on the day.

steps.title: Inspect each floor's condition (carpet pre-inspection per S100)
steps.detail: Inspect the actual condition of every floor. For carpet, ANSI/IICRC S100 treats pre-inspection as part of the job: identify fibre and construction, read the soil load, and find pre-existing damage, staining, delamination, prior repairs or wear patterns. For tile, note grout condition and whether the tile is sound; for vinyl, note the existing finish and substrate; for external surfaces, note the material and how fragile it is. Photograph and record what you find. S100 is a recognised standard of care, not law.

steps.title: Assess WHS hazards and plan controls
steps.detail: Identify slip, trip, electrical, chemical and manual-handling hazards across the job. Note where wet or freshly treated floors will need isolation and signage, where ventilation will be needed for strippers, sealers and presprays, and where high-pressure water is an injury risk. Prepare a SWMS/JSEA for any high-risk work you have identified, such as caustic vinyl strip-and-seal or pressure washing.

steps.title: Confirm chemical and SDS readiness
steps.detail: List every chemical you intend to bring and confirm you hold a current Safety Data Sheet — reviewed within the last 5 years, with its last-reviewed date on the sheet — for each one, in an accessible hazardous chemicals register (model WHS Regulation 346). Plan to correctly label any container you decant into. Where a catalogue item lacks a current SDS, obtain it from the supplier before it comes on site — do not invent or guess a code. Remember Victoria works under its own OHS Act and Regulations rather than the model WHS framework.

steps.title: Note slip-resistance targets and how you will verify them
steps.detail: Where a surface will change — particularly tile and grout, and vinyl after sealing — note any slip-resistance target the contract or site sets. Verification of an existing, in-service floor is measured under AS 4663 (as distinct from AS 4586, which classifies new pedestrian surfaces before installation). These standards classify and measure slip resistance only; they do not set cure or re-entry times. Do not quote a specific slip-rating number — consult HB 198 and the NCC for recommended classifications by location type.

steps.title: Plan run-off control for any pressure washing
steps.detail: If pressure washing is in scope, plan run-off control now. Locate the stormwater pits (which generally flow untreated to waterways — the drain is just for rain), plan containment with booms, socks, bunding or pit covers, plan recovery with a wet vacuum, and confirm with the water authority whether a trade-waste agreement is needed before you discharge wash water to sewer. In Victoria, EPA Victoria treats contaminated wash water as industrial waste. Arrange approvals before the job, not after.

steps.title: Record pre-existing damage and set realistic client expectations
steps.detail: Document existing damage, permanent staining and wear before you start, so a pre-existing problem is not later blamed on your visit — this protects you commercially. Flag realistic outcomes to the client for the method and soil load you are dealing with, and be deliberately careful with numbers: do not promise fixed dry, cure or re-entry times, which depend on the exact product and the conditions on the day.

steps.title: Produce the inspection record and method/scheduling plan
steps.detail: Pull the inspection into a written record that feeds the quote: surfaces and condition, hazards and controls, chemical and SDS readiness, slip-resistance and run-off plans, and a schedule that places periodic work in deep-clean or holiday windows and sequences any occupied-site work with realistic drying and isolation built in. Confirm the plan against the current contract specification and the site's own rules before you commit dates.

safety: Treat the pre-job inspection as work on a potentially occupied site: sign in, wear appropriate PPE, and stay clear of any live classroom or play activity.
Identify slip, trip, electrical, chemical and manual-handling hazards during the walk, and note where wet or treated floors will later need isolation and clear signage.
Confirm power is 230 V / 50 Hz through 10 A GPOs protected by an RCD/safety switch, and that every lead and machine is within its AS/NZS 3760 test-and-tag interval before it comes on site.

Record every chemical you intend to use and confirm a current Safety Data Sheet (reviewed within the last 5 years) for each, held in an accessible hazardous chemicals register (model WHS Regulation 346). Victoria operates under its own OHS Act and Regulations — check WorkSafe Victoria.
Keep the Poisons Information Centre number (13 11 26) with your SDS in case of exposure.
Prepare a SWMS/JSEA for the high-risk work identified at inspection, such as caustic strip-and-seal or pressure washing.
childSafe: Confirm each worker attending holds the correct state or territory Working With Children check, and complete any site child-safe induction — a WWC check is a starting point, not the whole picture.
Book the inspection and the work around occupancy: identify low-occupancy windows and note that periodic work belongs in deep-clean or term-break/holiday programs.

Map where children are and plan how the work zone will be physically separated, signed and kept off-limits while floors are wet or treated; keep children and pets away from equipment, leads and chemicals.
Note ventilation options for occupied rooms so strippers, sealers and presprays are used with fresh air moving away from children.
Favour lower-tox, child-safe product options for routine occupied work, reserving stronger chemistry for isolated deep-clean windows.
standards: Model WHS Act and Regulations (Safe Work Australia, 2011) as adopted by your state or territory; Victoria operates under its own OHS Act and Regulations.
Model WHS Regulation 346 (hazardous chemicals register); Schedule 11 manifest thresholds (generally not triggered by ordinary floor-care quantities).
AS/NZS 3760 (in-service inspection and testing of electrical equipment — test-and-tag) for leads and machines.
ANSI/IICRC S100 (S100-2021) as a recognised standard of care for carpet pre-inspection — guidance, not law. This course awards no IICRC CECs and is not an IICRC-accredited course.
AS 4586 (slip resistance of new pedestrian surfaces) and AS 4663 (slip resistance of existing/in-service surfaces); SA HB 198 and the NCC for recommended classifications.
National Quality Framework / NQS Quality Area 2 (childcare); state and territory Working With Children check legislation.
State EPA and water-authority trade-waste requirements where pressure washing is in scope.
documentation: A written pre-job inspection record: surfaces and areas in scope, occupancy and scheduling notes, and access arrangements.
Dated photographs and notes of pre-existing damage, staining and wear, to protect you commercially.
The chemicals list with a current SDS for each held in the register (Reg 346), including the decant-labelling plan.
Identified hazards and the SWMS/JSEA for high-risk tasks.
Any slip-resistance target and the plan to verify it under AS 4663; the run-off and trade-waste plan for any pressure washing.
The method and scheduling plan (routine versus periodic scope) feeding the quote, verified against the current contract specification and the site's own rules.
contentBodyHtml:
Service Procedure — Pre-Job Site & Floor Inspection

A school or childcare contract assumes you inspect before you quote. The pre-job inspection confirms the scope, checks access, power and water, reads the condition of every floor, and produces the dated record and readiness documentation the contract expects. Work through the steps in order and finish with the inspection record that feeds your quote and method statement.

  1. Confirm scope and the current contract specification. Separate routine scope (light, frequent cleaning) from periodic scope (hard-floor strip-and-seal, carpet hot water extraction) and list every surface in play — carpet, tile and grout, vinyl and resilient floors, and external hard surfaces. Published standards such as School Infrastructure NSW are directional only; the binding detail is the current contract specification, so verify against it.
  2. Confirm site credentials and child-safe requirements before you attend. Child-related work generally requires a Working With Children check, administered per state and territory (NSW WWCC, VIC Working with Children Check, QLD Blue Card, ACT WWVP) and generally not transferable. Confirm each worker holds the correct current check and complete any site child-safe induction.
  3. Book the inspection around occupancy. Note the low-occupancy windows and term-break or holiday periods periodic work will need, and confirm access with the site contact rather than assuming a window is clear.
  4. Walk the site and map rooms, zones and access. Note occupancy patterns and entry/exit routes, identify where children spend time, and note where you can sequence the work room-by-room so each space can be treated, dried and reopened while you move on.
  5. Check services and utilities — water and power. Confirm water access and waste-water disposal points. Confirm power is 230 V / 50 Hz through 10 A GPOs protected by an RCD/safety switch, and that every lead and machine is within its AS/NZS 3760 test-and-tag interval. For pressure washing, locate the stormwater pits and intended discharge point now, not on the day.
  6. Inspect each floor's condition (carpet pre-inspection per S100). For carpet, ANSI/IICRC S100 treats pre-inspection as part of the job: identify fibre and construction, read the soil load, and find pre-existing damage, staining, delamination or wear. For tile, note grout condition; for vinyl, note the existing finish; for external surfaces, note the material and how fragile it is. Photograph and record what you find.
  7. Assess WHS hazards and plan controls. Identify slip, trip, electrical, chemical and manual-handling hazards, note where wet floors will need isolation and signage and where ventilation is needed, and prepare a SWMS/JSEA for high-risk work such as caustic strip-and-seal or pressure washing.
  8. Confirm chemical and SDS readiness. List every chemical you will bring and confirm a current SDS (reviewed within the last 5 years) for each, in an accessible register (model WHS Regulation 346). Plan to correctly label any decanted container. Where an item lacks a current SDS, obtain it from the supplier — do not guess a code. Victoria uses its own OHS framework.
  9. Note slip-resistance targets and how you will verify them. Where a surface will change (tile and grout, and vinyl after sealing), note any target slip class. An existing, in-service floor is measured under AS 4663 (AS 4586 classifies new surfaces before installation). These standards classify and measure only — they do not set re-entry times. Consult HB 198 and the NCC for recommended classifications rather than quoting a rating.
  10. Plan run-off control for any pressure washing. Locate the stormwater pits (they generally flow untreated to waterways — the drain is just for rain), plan containment and recovery, and confirm with the water authority whether a trade-waste agreement is needed before discharging wash water to sewer. In Victoria, EPA Victoria treats contaminated wash water as industrial waste.
  11. Record pre-existing damage and set realistic client expectations. Document existing damage, permanent staining and wear before you start so a pre-existing problem is not later blamed on your visit. Flag realistic outcomes, and do not promise fixed dry, cure or re-entry times — they depend on the product and the conditions.
  12. Produce the inspection record and method/scheduling plan. Pull it into a written record that feeds the quote: surfaces and condition, hazards and controls, chemical and SDS readiness, slip-resistance and run-off plans, and a schedule that places periodic work in deep-clean or holiday windows and sequences occupied-site work with drying and isolation built in. Confirm against the current contract specification and the site's rules before committing dates.

Safety

  • Sign in, wear appropriate PPE, and stay clear of live classroom or play activity during the walk.
  • Identify slip, trip, electrical, chemical and manual-handling hazards, and note where wet or treated floors will need isolation and signage.
  • Confirm power is 230 V / 50 Hz through 10 A GPOs on an RCD/safety switch, and that leads and machines are within their AS/NZS 3760 test-and-tag interval.
  • Confirm a current SDS (within 5 years) for every chemical, in an accessible register (model WHS Regulation 346); check WorkSafe Victoria for VIC contracts. Keep Poisons Information 13 11 26 with your SDS.
  • Prepare a SWMS/JSEA for high-risk work such as caustic strip-and-seal or pressure washing.

Child-safe

  • Confirm each worker holds the correct state or territory Working With Children check, plus any site child-safe induction — the WWC check is a starting point, not the whole picture.
  • Book the inspection and the work around occupancy; periodic work belongs in deep-clean or term-break/holiday programs.
  • Plan how the work zone will be physically separated, signed and kept off-limits while floors are wet or treated; keep children and pets away from equipment, leads and chemicals.
  • Note ventilation options so strippers, sealers and presprays are used with fresh air moving away from children.
  • Favour lower-tox, child-safe products for routine occupied work; reserve stronger chemistry for isolated deep-clean windows.

Standards

  • Model WHS Act and Regulations (Safe Work Australia, 2011) as adopted by your state or territory; Victoria uses its own OHS Act and Regulations.
  • Model WHS Regulation 346 (hazardous chemicals register); Schedule 11 manifest thresholds (generally not triggered by ordinary floor-care quantities).
  • AS/NZS 3760 (test-and-tag) for electrical leads and equipment.
  • ANSI/IICRC S100 (S100-2021) as a recognised standard of care for carpet pre-inspection — guidance, not law. No IICRC CECs are awarded and this is not an IICRC-accredited course.
  • AS 4586 (new pedestrian surfaces) and AS 4663 (existing/in-service surfaces); SA HB 198 and the NCC for recommended classifications.
  • NQS Quality Area 2 (childcare); state and territory Working With Children check legislation; state EPA and water-authority trade-waste requirements.

Document this

  • A written pre-job inspection record: surfaces and areas in scope, occupancy and scheduling notes, and access arrangements.
  • Dated photographs and notes of pre-existing damage, staining and wear.
  • The chemicals list with a current SDS for each in the register (Reg 346), including the decant-labelling plan.
  • Identified hazards and the SWMS/JSEA for high-risk tasks.
  • Any slip-resistance target and the plan to verify it under AS 4663; the run-off and trade-waste plan for pressure washing.
  • The method and scheduling plan (routine versus periodic scope) feeding the quote, verified against the current contract specification.
